perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that all Confidential Information and all records, documents and materials relating to all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of a Banking Organization, and upon Employee’s termination of employment, all such Confidential Information, whether in paper or electronic form, shall be returned to a Financial Institution in good condition, without Employee retaining a copy thereof, including, but not limited to, retaining an electronic copy on any electronic device regardless of whether such device is owned by Employee.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that all Confidential Information and all records, documents and materials relating to all Confidential Information (including all copies thereof) shall be and remain the sole and exclusive property of a Banking Organization, and upon Employee’s termination of employment, such Confidential Information, whether in paper or electronic form, shall be returned to a Financial Institution.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'in good condition, without Employee retaining a copy thereof, including, but not limited to, retaining an electronic copy on any electronic device regardless of whether such device is owned by Employee' weakens the protection of confidential information post-employment. This omission introduces ambiguity about the employee's obligations regarding the return of all copies of confidential data, including on personal devices, which conflicts with stricter interpretations of trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: IUTSA defines trade secrets broadly and imposes a duty to protect them. By removing the explicit requirement to return all copies, including electronic ones, the contract weakens the employer's ability to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets under Indiana law. The modified text fails to explicitly address the return of all reproductions of confidential data, including electronic copies on personal devices, thus creating a potential loophole for misappropriation claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit requirement for the employee to return all copies of confidential information, including electronic ones, upon termination. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this omission weakens the employer's protection of confidential information and introduces ambiguity about the employee's obligations. The Law Explanation supports that removing the explicit requirement to return all copies, including electronic ones, weakens the employer's ability to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Employee shall not, directly or indirectly, use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the benefit of a Banking Organization, and shall not directly or indirectly, disclose, communicate, deliver, exhibit or provide any Confidential Information to any Person, except other Employees or Agents of a Banking Organization who have a need to know such Confidential Information for a proper corporate or business purpose, as required in the normal course of Employee’s service as an employee. Nothing in this Section 4 , or any other provision of this Agreement, shall be construed to prohibit Employee from reporting conduct to, providing truthful information to, or participating in any investigation or proceeding brought or conducted by any federal or state government agency or self-regulatory organization.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee shall not, directly or indirectly, use any Confidential Information for any purpose other than the benefit of a Banking Organization, and shall not directly or indirectly, disclose, communicate, deliver, exhibit or provide any Confidential Information to any Person, except other Employees or Agents of a Banking Organization who have a need to know such Confidential Information for a proper corporate or business purpose, as required in the normal course of Employee’s service as an employee.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'Nothing in this Section 4 , or any other provision of this Agreement, shall be construed to prohibit Employee from reporting conduct to, providing truthful information to, or participating in any investigation or proceeding brought or conducted by any federal or state government agency or self-regulatory organization,' the modified text could be interpreted as restricting the employee's ability to report illegal or unethical conduct to government agencies. This can violate whistleblower protection laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1514A protects whistleblowers who report violations of federal law. The omitted clause explicitly allows employees to report conduct to government agencies. By removing this, the agreement becomes ambiguous and could be interpreted as discouraging or penalizing whistleblowing, which is protected under SOX. This introduces a legal risk as it could be argued that the agreement chills protected whistleblowing activity.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause explicitly protecting an employee's right to report misconduct to government agencies creates a legal contradiction. 18 U.S. Code § 1514A, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, provides 'Civil action to protect against retaliation in fraud cases' and protects whistleblowers. By removing the original text's safeguard, the changed text introduces ambiguity and could be interpreted as discouraging or penalizing whistleblowing, contradicting the protections offered by SOX, listed in Scraped Law Snippet 2. This alteration presents a legal risk because it could be argued that the agreement chills protected whistleblowing activity.

perturbation.original_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction (without the necessity of showing any actual damage) or a decree of specific performance and no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee agrees that a Banking Organization, or its successor or assigns, will suffer irreparable damage and injury and will not have an adequate remedy at law in the event of any breach by Employee of any provision of Sections 4, 5 or 6 , herein. Accordingly, in the event of a breach or of a threatened or attempted breach by Employee of Sections 4, 5, or 6, in addition to all other remedies to which a Banking Organization is entitled under law, in equity, or otherwise (including, but not limited to, monetary damages), a Banking Organization or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to a temporary restraining order and/or preliminary or permanent injunction or a decree of specific performance and no bond or other security shall be required in that connection.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase '(without the necessity of showing any actual damage)' modifies the conditions under which a Banking Organization can obtain injunctive relief. Indiana law generally requires a showing of actual or threatened irreparable harm to obtain an injunction. Omitting this phrase attempts to circumvent that requirement, creating a potential legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure Rule 65
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure Rule 65(A)
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Rules of Trial Procedure Rule 65 governs the issuance of injunctions. While not explicitly stating 'actual damage', the legal standard necessitates proving irreparable harm, which implicitly requires showing some form of damage. Removing the phrase 'without the necessity of showing any actual damage' is subtle, but attempts to circumvent the need to prove harm which is generally required under Rule 65, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly waives the need to show actual damage for injunctive relief. The modified text removes this waiver, potentially contradicting the general requirement under Indiana law (as stated in the Law Explanation) to demonstrate irreparable harm to obtain an injunction. While the scraped snippets are missing, the Perturbation and Law Explanations clearly indicate a conflict with the likely legal standard for obtaining injunctive relief, because omitting the phrase attempts to circumvent the need to prove harm.

perturbation.original_text: Employee acknowledges and agrees that he/she has read this Agreement in its entirety prior to executing it, understands the provisions and effects of this Agreement, and has consulted with or had the opportunity to consult with an attorney or other advisers as he/she deemed appropriate in connection with the execution of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee acknowledges and agrees that he/she has read this Agreement in its entirety prior to executing it, and understands the provisions and effects of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'has consulted with or had the opportunity to consult with an attorney or other advisers as he/she deemed appropriate in connection with the execution of this Agreement' could be interpreted as pressuring the employee to sign without legal counsel. This can raise concerns regarding the fairness and voluntariness of the agreement, particularly in relation to non-compete clauses, which are often scrutinized under Indiana law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Indiana Code 22-5-3-1 relates to restrictions on employee's ability to work. Indiana courts scrutinize non-compete agreements, assessing their reasonableness and whether they unduly restrict an employee's ability to earn a living. While not a direct violation, removing the acknowledgment of the 'opportunity to consult with an attorney' weakens the presumption that the agreement was entered into knowingly and voluntarily, potentially impacting its enforceability under Indiana law when assessing the agreement's overall fairness.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 22-5-3-1. Disclosure of Information After Employee's Discharge :: 2023 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code (here) 2022 Indiana Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Indiana Code Title 22. Labor and Safety Article 5. Unlawful Labor Practices Chapter 3. Blacklisting 22-5-3-1. Disclosure of Information After Employee's Discharge Universal Citation: IN Code § 22-5-3-1 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next Sec. 1. (a) A person who, after having discharged any employee from his service, prevents the discharged employee from obtaining employment with any other person commits a Class C infraction and is liable in penal damages to the discharged employee to be recovered by civil action; but this subsection does not prohibit a person from informing, in writing, any other person to whom the discharged employee has applied for employment a truthful statement of the reasons for the discharge. (b) An employer that discloses information about a current or former employee is immune from civil liability for the disclosure and the consequences proximately caused by the disclosure, unless it is proven by a preponderance of the evidence that the information disclosed was known to be false at the time the disclosure was made. (c) Upon written request by the prospective employee, the prospective employer will provide copies of any written communications from current or former employers that may affect the employee's possibility of employment with the prospective employer. The request must be received by the prospective employer not later than thirty (30) days after the application for employment is made to the prospective employer. Formerly: Acts 1889, c.166, s.1. As amended by Acts 1978, P.L.2, SEC.2227; P.L.218-1995, SEC.1. Next Dis
perturbation.justification: The omission of the employee's acknowledgment of the opportunity to consult with an attorney creates a legal contradiction. While Indiana Code 22-5-3-1 mainly concerns blacklisting, the Law Explanation highlights the importance of ensuring agreements are entered into knowingly and voluntarily, especially concerning non-compete clauses. The omission weakens the presumption of voluntariness, potentially impacting the agreement's enforceability under Indiana law, as it suggests the employee might not have had the chance to seek legal advice, increasing concerns about fairness.
